One of the players established that their memories were wiped after they finished their years of enslavement doing a corporation's bidding. Make a thread : They were a debt collector, through more or less moral means. Add clues such as people recognizing them or having vendettas against them, ...

Remember to review your threads in between sessions. Have their clues been exhausted ? Have the players unravelled them yet ? Should you remove some ? Should you add some ? When you think the campaign arc is starting to close up, do not add new threads and try as much as you can to give clues to resolve the last ones. When all threads are exhausted, players should clearly see the stakes and finish the campaign with the threats.

docs.title: Threats
docs.text:
Threats are a way to keep track of coherent sets of obstacles and menaces governing play. Threats can be an organization plotting in the shadows, an individual pursuing its own mission, societal unrest turned rebellion, an artificial intelligence awakening, ...

Threats have a Motive. Their motive may be their conscious goal or their impulse. Their motive describe why the threat do what they do. A corporation may try to eliminate a rival and take their place, local authorities may try to maintain order and secure their power, a rising politician may try to gain power, an alien planet may try to manifest its danger, ...

Threats have Threat complications which are fictional consequences triggered using the same rules as the general MC complications. Theses complications are designed to flesh out the threat in the fiction. For instance, a corporation may acquire an hostage, a criminal organization may put a price on the head of someone, a derelict ship may suddenly depressurize parts of its facilities, ... Use the threat complications below but make sure to adapt them to the flavor of your specific threat.

A threat may have an associated Threat Clock if the threat is progressive. A threat clock may have as many unfilled tracks as you like (from 3 for a fast developing threat to 6 to a slowly developing threat). These tracks are filled along the threat's development whenever you make a complication from this threat. You may associate complications to specific tracks of the clock in a progressively developing way.

If the last track of a threat clock is filled, the threat fully manifests. A threat that fully manifest should be game changing : people may massively die, governments may be overthrown, habitats may be destroyed, robots may be uprising, etc... This last track is a particularly hard complication and should profoundly affect the story in a permanent way. Don't be lenient on threats that fully realize. Such profound changes should always be justified in the fiction and the previous tracks should naturally announce and progress towards the threat full manifestation. It is very important to have these final manifestations feel natural during play. If a threat fully manifests, this threat may disappear and spawn new sub threats or change its motive and complications. Think about the long term consequences in order to adapt your threats.

Threats may be stopped or changed if the fiction demands it. If the oyabun of the local yakuza organization is killed, disgraced or captured, the yakuza threat may disappear, change or reset. If the threat change, you may want to change its motive and complications. If the threat has a clock, delete it, modify it or reset it accordingly.

docs.title: Threat generation
docs.text: Here are some generic threat types, motives and complications that you can use to build your threats. Always remember to add flavor to your threats by customizing their motives and complications.

docs.title: Organizations and individuals
docs.text: Organizations and individual threats are the most common threats. Make sure to have at least one or two. Don't hesitate to be granular with organizations and to create individual threats inside organizations threats. A corporation is surely a threat but an ambitious executive that's part of this corporation can also have its own motives and interests, sometimes conflicting with its organization interests, sometimes aligning with them.

docs.title: Organizations
docs.text:
• Army : To secure operational control. To win the war. To hold their position.
  • Authorities : To maintain their power and mitigate rebellion. To track and punish criminal activity.
  • Cabal : To accomplish their vision.
  • Corporation : To profit, no matter the cost. To gain a new market. To maintain their economic dominion. To acquire a special asset.
  • Mafia : To profit from and maintain their territory and protect their own.
  • Rebels : To overthrow the current authorities.
  • Terrorists : To impose their ideals through terror.

docs.title: Individual
docs.text:
• Avenger : To get revenge.
  • Investigator : To reveal the truth.
  • Missionary : To further their mission.
  • Runner : To lay low and disappear.
  • Plotter : To conquer and secure power.
  • Spy : To acquire intel and secure assets.

docs.title: Organizations and individual complications
docs.text:
• Acquire an asset for deployment or leverage.
  • Acquire intel about someone or something.
  • Ask for a meeting.
  • Be there when things happen.
  • Capture a fork for information extraction or leverage.
  • Capture someone for torture, punishment or leverage.
  • Deploy a memetic attack.
  • Deploy an asset.
  • Deploy surveillance measures.
  • Do a discreet attack.
  • Do a frontal attack.
  • Make threats.
  • Meet with another threat.
  • Propose a temporary alliance.
  • Publish or selectively communicate damaging information.
  • Set up a trap.

docs.title: Environments
docs.text:
Environmental threats should be used to flesh out the dangers of a specific place.

  • Alien planet : To manifest weirdness and danger.
  • Derelict ship or space station : To malfunction.
  • Extreme conditions : To manifest harshness.
  • Nanotech catastrophe : To spread.
  • War zone : To manifest horror and alienation.

docs.title: Environmental complications
docs.text:
These complications are highly generic and should be customized accordingly.

  • Block a way.
  • Manifest an obstacle.
  • Manifest beauty.
  • Manifest danger.
  • Open a way.

docs.title: Habitats
docs.text: Habitat threats are to flesh out the habitat in which the players are. If you're playing in an urban environment, make sure to have an habitat threat for it.

docs.title: Habitat complications
docs.text:
• Manifest its architecture.
  • Manifest criminality.
  • Manifest local memetics.
  • Manifest poorness.
  • Manifest social delineation.
  • Manifest the local mesh.
  • Manifest wealth.

docs.title: Others
docs.text: There are other kinds of threats. What about a highly mutable and intelligent virus ? What about an awakening singularity ? What about alien diplomats with obscure intentions ? What about alien environments that seem to act intentionally ? Don't hesitate to create weird threats with original complications and motives to flesh them out.

docs.title: Endure/Overcome +Approach
docs.text:
When you overcome an obstacle or push through danger, choose with the MC if you do it with :

  • Speed and power : roll +Kinesics.
  • Awareness and vigilance : roll +Sensors.
  • Anticipation and computation : roll +Process.
  • Sociability and manipulation : roll +Memetics.

Success

You do it.

Partial

You do it but it's not pretty. You'll either get a mitigated result, a choice between two complications or an unforeseen consequence. MC's choice.

docs.title: Boost/Disrupt
docs.text: When you help or impede another player and it's time to roll some dice, give them +-1d-forward, as appropriate. You are now entangled in the consequences of their move.

docs.title: Get in touch +Memetics
docs.text:
Once per session, you can declare a contact. Say who they are, what they do, where they usually do it and roll +Memetics.

Success

Anything for a friend. They owe you one, why?

Partial

Just business. They're around, but they don't work for free. Offer help or pay up.

docs.title: Query
docs.text:
When you're combing through a large dataset, using a search engine or analyzing a filesystem, looking for answers, you can ask the MC any question and they will answer you honestly, provided the data contains this information. Querying the Grid is always an option but it will only yield public results.

If the data is seriously encrypted or laced with aggressive malware, the MC will tell you what you need to go through to clear it.

docs.title: Virtual reality
docs.text: When you make a move in a simulspace, and the move still makes some sense outside of the meat world, replace mentions of +Sensors and +Kinesics with +Process instead.

docs.title: Analyze behavior +Memetics
docs.text:
When you're analyzing someone's behavior throughout an interaction, roll +Memetics.

Success

Hold 3.

Partial

Hold 1.

Miss

Hold 1 but your target analyzed you as well (MC may ask questions).

For the duration of the interaction you can spend your hold 1 for 1 to ask one of the following questions :

  • Are they honest ?
  • What do they want ?
  • What are they feeling right now ?
  • Which memes do they adhere to ?
  • How could I get them to ___ ?

On a success or partial, you have +1d-forward if you act on the answers.

docs.title: Assess a situation +Sensors
docs.text:
When you assess a situation, roll +Sensors.

Success

Ask 3.

Partial

Ask 1.

Miss

Ask 1 anyways but the MC will give you a soft complication.

Questions :

  • What technology is at play here ?
  • What's a potential complication ?
  • What's hidden here ?
  • How ___ is vulnerable to me ?
  • What's the best way in / out / past / through ?
  • What's the biggest threat here ?

On a success or partial, you have +1d-forward if you act on the answers.

docs.title: Convince +Memetics
docs.text:
When you argue, fast talk, persuade or seduce someone to do something feasible or to believe something plausible, roll +Memetics.

Success

They do it or believe you until something tangible contradicts your reasoning.

Partial

They do it or believe you if you first promise to do something or give them a credible corroboration or warranty.

On PCs :

Success

Both.

  • They gain 1 XP if they accept
  • They take -1d-forward if they refuse.

Partial

Choose 1 above.

Miss

They have +1d-forward against you.

docs.title: Deploy violence +Kinesics
docs.text:
When you deploy violence, say what you want and roll +Kinesics.

Success

You inflict harm and choose 1 :

  • You suffer no harm.
  • You take definitive control of someone/something/some place but suffer harm.

Partial

You inflict harm, suffer harm and the MC gives a complication.

Miss

You suffer harm and the MC gives a complication.

docs.title: Infiltrate +Sensors
docs.text:
When you're sneaking in places you don't belong, roll +Sensors.

Success

You go unnoticed and have +1d-forward.

Partial

You go unnoticed but the MC chooses 1 :

  • You attract minor attention.
  • You leave something behind.
  • You take something with you.
  • You have to overcome an obstacle.

Miss

You're detected but you have a small window to prevent the alert to be transmitted.

docs.title: Intimidate +Kinesics
docs.text:
When you want someone to do something under threat of violence, tell them what you want them to do and what you'll do to them if they don't cooperate, then roll +Kinesics.

Success

They choose between :

  • Force your hand and suck it up.
  • Cave and do what you want.

Partial

They may also choose :

  • Run away.
  • Try to diffuse the situation.
  • Prepare for conflict.

docs.title: Suffer trauma +Trauma
docs.text:
When you suffer trauma, add it to your total then roll +Trauma.

"Success"

You lose control of yourself (MC says what you do). Memories optional.

"Partial"

Choose 1 :

  • You have -1d-ongoing until you can chill out.
  • You miss something.

"Miss"

You internalize the trauma and push forward.

In any case, the MC gives you a trauma complication.

When you reach 6-trauma, you lose it. Your character may take a last stand but after that they become a NPC. They may still have an old backup of themselves with less trauma, who knows ?

docs.title: Suffer harm +Harm
docs.text:
When you suffer harm, roll +Harm suffered.

"Success"

Choose 1 :

  • You're out of action : unconscious, trapped, lost, glitched or panicked.
  • One of your aug is destroyed, MC's choice.

"Partial"

Choose 1 :

  • You miss something.
  • You're put in a spot.
  • One of your aug or locomotion mode is broken (you say which).
  • Take -1d-ongoing until you can rest.

"Miss"

You endure the damage.

If you have a total of 6 or more harm or if your shell dies, it's time to take a look at your Resurrection insurance.

docs.title: Design +Process Restricted
docs.text:
When you want to build or modify something, the MC will tell you what you need to make it happen by picking as many options from the following list as makes sense :

  • It will take a 1, 2 or 3 actions to complete.
  • You will need a worskpace.
  • You will need to spend some cred to cover the basics.
  • You will need special materials, tools and/or data.
  • You will need extensive danger management measures.
  • You will need the help of ___.

When you have everything you need, roll +Process.

Success

Just as planned, whatever you made, you made it right.

Partial

It's a beta, it works but pick 1 :

  • One of the features doesn't work properly. What is it?
  • It's more fragile than you wish. Add 2-use.
  • You need something more to make it work. The MC will tell you what. Add destroyed until you get it.
  • It works first try. Add dangerous.

Miss

You have a barely working prototype, it should do the job but it's unpredictable. When you use it, Overcome with +Process to see what happens.

docs.title: Gig +Rep
docs.text:
When you're looking to make some money in your spare time, choose a faction with which you have a Rep of 0 or more and roll +Rep.

Success

They could really use someone with your talents right now. Tell us what they needed you for. You make 5-cred and +1 Rep.

Partial

You've got options, a well-paying job or something that will get you noticed. Choose if you make 5-cred or 3-cred and +1 Rep. Figure out the details of the gig you end up doing with the MC.

Miss

There's something they need done but it's problematic to say the least. The MC tells you what it is. If you do it, make 5-cred and lose -1 Rep with another faction. If you don't, you find a crappy gig elsewhere, make 3-cred and tell us why your character swore they're never doing that again.

docs.title: Investigate +Process
docs.text:
When you investigate a person, group or location, roll +Process.

Success

Ask 3.

Partial

Ask 1.

Miss

Ask 1 anyway but the MC gives a complication.

Questions :

  • Where is ___ ?
  • How is ___ secured ?
  • To whom ___ may be valuable ?
  • To whom or what is ___ related ?
  • Who owned or employed ___ ?
  • Are ___ and ___ related ? If yes, how ?
  • What doesn't fit about ___ ?

docs.title: Reshell +Trauma
docs.text:
When you wake up in a new shell, roll +Trauma.

Success

It goes as well as it could, your Ellipsis action is refunded. What feels familiar about this shell?

Partial

It's the mother of all blackouts, something's off and you need to do go through the paces. What's the first thing you do to feel right in this shell?

Miss

This is not your body. Suffer 1-trauma. What's wrong and what do you have to do to yourself to fix it?

Then, because it's an important event, take the spotlight for a bit to show the group what rebirth is like for you.

If you're out of ideas, consider the following prompts:

  • "What's your first thought or impression when waking up ?"
  • "How does your character experience discontinuity ?"
  • "What about this shell feels different to you ?"

docs.title: Diverge +Forks
docs.text:
At the beginning of the session, upon ellipsis or if the MC asks for it, roll the number of forks you currently have (your original self included).

"Success"

The writing is on the wall. Add +3 to your Divergence clock.

"Partial"

Small signs, unusual behaviors. Add +1 to the Divergence clock.

"Miss"

You're you and the other you's are you, too. keep narrative control. For now.

docs.title: Fork
docs.text:
The first time you make a fork of yourself, you gain a Divergence clock with 4 segments unfilled. You can play that fork as if it's a full second character. Consider making a sheet for them if they start gaining XP or they reshell.

Whenever that clock is full, for any reason, one of you has grown appart. Pick one of your forks, you lose narrative control over their actions and they become an NPC. How they act towards you and yours is up to the MC now. It's usually a mess, but it's not written in stone.

When that happens, if you've still got forks left, reset the divergence clock to 2.

docs.title: Discord and Unity
docs.text: When you pay tribute or make yourself useful to a faction, gain +1 Rep with them. When what you did hurt a faction, remove -1 or -2 Rep with them depending on the damage done. When you publicly help a faction with enemies, lose -1 Rep with one of them.

docs.title: Favors +Rep
docs.text:
When you approach a member of a faction looking for a favor, roll +Rep.

Success

They are feeling gracious, get 1 Favor no strings attached.

Partial

They'll help out, but you owe them. get 1 Favor right now but they'll ask something from you. If you don't do it, lose 1 Rep with them.

Miss

They get contractual. They ask something from you right now. If you do it, get 1 Favor. If you don't, lose -1 Rep with them.

Favors :

  • Ask a question from the Investigate move.
  • Get a gear of value equal to their Tier or less.
  • Get a temporary squad or expert of their Tier.
  • Ask for a special device, service or vehicle.

docs.title: Resurrection insurance
docs.text:
When your shell is destroyed and your resurrection insurance pays off...

... if you have a Lifestyle 0, get a basic infoshell.

... if you have a Lifestyle 1, 2 or 3, choose as many as your Lifestyle :

  • The new shell has the same augs (or of same value) as your previous one (else it has -tier-3 and the MC may choose to change some).
  • You choose if the shell has a downside: tracked, previous owner, unresponsive, spurious perception, addiction, wild controls, psycho-affective trouble, chronic pain, ...
  • You choose the shell's type (bioshell or synshell) and look.

All unselected options are for the MC to detail. You may refuse the MC detailing an option by decreasing your Lifestyle by 1 for each option refused.

Then you probably need to Reshell during the next ellipsis.

docs.title: Resist hacking +Process
docs.text:
When you're getting hacked and warnings start popping up everywhere, roll +Process.

Success

You manage to contain the attack and you gather some useful data on the assailant.

Partial

Choose 1 :

  • You suffer 2-harm ap
  • One of your augs or connected items gets broken (MC's choice).
  • Your sensors saturates and you miss something important.
  • They get info on you and what you may know.
  • You're glitched : -1 ongoing until you can recalibrate.

Miss

MC chooses 1 :

  • You suffer 4-harm ap
  • You suffer 1-trauma
  • One of your augs gets destroyed
  • They forknap you.
  • Your shell is their puppet : Overcome with +Process to gain back control or not...

docs.title: Freeform and ellipsis
docs.text:
The game is segmented into two "modes of play" : freeform and ellipsis.

By default, the game is in freeform mode. What this means is that actions and their durations are relatively flexible. You can roleplay an interaction in real time, cut to another scene, skip uninteresting bits, etc... In freeform mode, you may use all moves except for the ellipsis moves.

In ellipsis mode, time passes. This means two things : first the PC have access to special actions in the form of ellipsis moves and second, the MC makes moves allowing them to explore long term consequences and evolutions.

Ellipsis mode :

An ellipsis phase starts when all players agree. During an ellipsis, make sure all of the following happen :

  • PCs with a divergence clock make the Diverge move.
  • If your lifestyle is 0-cred, you may have problems.
  • Each PC makes an ellipsis move. For each PC making an ellipsis move, the MC makes a move and/or ticks a clock (they may not make a move or tick a clock with the same threat except if PCs ask for additional ellipsis moves).
  • PCs may make as many additional ellipsis moves as they want but for each one, the MC makes an additional move and/or ticks a clock.
  • PCs make the Checkout moves.

PC ellipsis action choices :

  • Hit the clinic and get a new aug
  • Investigate
  • Reshell
  • Get a gig
  • Design

Some expertise moves count as ellipsis actions :

  • Hack the world [Hacking]
  • Make a case [Investigation]
  • Propaganda [Memegineering]
  • Accelerated psychotherapy [Psychosurgery]
  • Experimental psychosurgery [Psychosurgery]
  • Planning ahead [Specops]

docs.title: Factions
docs.text: Factions represent groups of transhumans with common interests and values. In Silicon Dust, factions are the social environment in which the PCs will evolve. Whether they want it or not, they'll be a part in the faction game.

docs.title: Rep
docs.text:
Rep (reputation) is a PC mechanic that represents the level of sympathy or hostility a character has with a faction. The PCs are required to track their Rep with every relevant faction. Rep can go from -1 to +3. A -1 Rep represents that this faction considers you an enemy. A +0 Rep represents neutrality. A positive Rep represents friendliness.

Gaining and loosing Rep with a faction occurs mainly as the consequence of the Faction game move but also can occur as a consequence of other moves or at MC's discretion.

docs.title: Faction game
docs.text:
When you do something helpful for or give something to a faction, gain +1 Rep with them. When you hurt a faction, remove -1 or -2 Rep with them depending on the gravity of your offense. When you publicly help the enemy of a faction, remove -1 rep from an enemy of that faction

Rep can also be used to make the Favors move. This move allows a PC to roll its Rep as base dice pool to get things from friendly factions.

docs.title: Favors +Rep
docs.text:
When you want a favor from a faction, choose a faction with which you have at least 0 Rep and roll +Rep.

Success

Choose 1 Favor for free.

Partial

Choose 1 Favor right now but they ask something from you. If you don't do it, loose 1 Rep with them.

Miss

They ask something from you right now. If you do it, choose 1 Favor. If you don't, loose -1 Rep with them.

Favors :

  • Ask a question from the Investigate move.
  • Get a gear of value equal to their Tier or less.
  • Get a temporary squad or expert of their Tier.
  • Ask for a special device, service or vehicle.

In any case, you may spend Rep, 1 for 1, to have another Favor.

The Gig move is also rolled with Rep as base dice pool.

docs.title: Gig +Rep Ellipsis
docs.text:
When you get a gig, choose a faction with which you have a Rep of 0 or more and roll +Rep.

Success

The faction gives you a good gig (you detail). You make 5-cred and +1 Rep.

Partial

The faction gives you a gig (you and MC details). Choose if you make 5-cred or 3-cred and +1 Rep.

Miss

The faction gives you a problematic gig (MC details). If you do it, you make 5-cred and loose -1 Rep with another faction. If you don't, you find a crappy gig elsewhere, make 3-cred and tell us how the gig was crappy.

docs.title: Tier
docs.text:
Factions have a Tier level ranging from 0 to IV. (V is the Conglomerate, VI is beyond transhumanity). Their Tier level represent their power in the faction game. It determines who they can bite and who can bite them. It is also used to determine what they can generate as a Favor and is an indicator to do fortune rolls.

A faction may only attack or undermine a faction with a Tier equal to its own + 1 or less. Rarely factions may loose or gain Tier as important events unfolds in fiction or as the consequence of moves.

Factions can be composed of sub-factions (their Tier is at least 1 lower than the mother faction).

docs.title: Factions are threats
docs.text: For the MC, factions are threats. As such they may have a threat clock, they can do faction complications, ... You may custom their faction complications by detailing special complication that fits the faction theme, motivations and aesthetic.

docs.title: Faction generator
docs.text:
When you need to create a faction, you may use this generator.

Choose at least 1 faction type:

  • Hypercorp.
  • Federation.
  • Freeform society.
  • Isolated community.
  • Outcasts.
  • Secret network.
  • State.
  • Religious cult.
  • Crime syndicate.

Choose 1 to 3 characteristics:

  • Anarcho-capitalist.
  • Anarcho-communist.
  • Aristocracy.
  • Corpocracy.
  • Authoritarian.
  • Cast system.
  • Criminal.
  • Direct democracy.
  • Exhuman.
  • Hive-mind.
  • Media and journalism.
  • Militarized.
  • Neoluddite.
  • Religious.
  • Representative democracy.
  • Scientific.

What are their resources (choose as many as their Tier) :

  • A Gate.
  • Anonymity.
  • Army and armaments.
  • Art and entertainment.
  • Communication networks.
  • Cryptocurrency.
  • Freedom.
  • Good contacts and relations.
  • High end engineering.
  • Intelligence and surveillance.
  • Knowledge and R&D.
  • Raw materials.
  • Social care.
  • Spatial fleet.
  • Tourism and luxury.
  • Transparency.
  • Vegetation.
  • Shell technology.

Where are they located (choose 1 or 2):

  • They are in multiple habitats.
  • They are nomad / drifters.
  • Mercury
  • Venus
  • Earth (Moon)
  • Mars (Phobos, Deimos)
  • Asteroid belt (Vesta, Juno, Ceres, Pallas, ...)
  • Jupiter (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto)
  • Saturn (Enceladus, Tethys, Rhea, Titan, Hyperion, ...)
  • Uranus (Miranda, Ariel, ...)
  • Neptune (Triton)
  • In the Oort cloud.
  • On the Grid (where are their servers though ?)
  • Beyond the gates (which system ?)

docs.title: Faction sheet
docs.text:
Name

Type : ...

Characteristics : ...

Resources : ...

Location(s) : ...

Clocks : - ...

Complications : - ...

docs.title: First session
docs.text:
1. The MC chooses a setting and announces it to the players.

  2. The Players choose their archetypes.

  3. While players fill in their character sheets, they create elements of worldbuilding. The MC should ask questions and submit suggestions to flesh out the universe. Players and the MC should co-create the starting habitat the same way.

  4. Players create their connections to other players.

  5. Players introduce their characters in a few scenes to set the tone and generate exposition. MC should ask numerous questions to players and introduce some elements.

  6. At the end of the first session, the MC should compile its notes and start creating threats and threads based on what was established during the first session.

docs.title: Choosing a playbook
docs.text: Just pass the playbooks and let the players choose their favourite. It's important that

docs.title: Trauma
docs.text:
The digital mind freed us from corporeal death. Our physical integrity matters less and less as we reshell when we die. But while physical harm has easy fixes, our traumas, on the contrary, stay with us.

Trauma represents last longing psychological harm and differs from traditional harm in several ways.

First, it can't be easily healed. Second, you keep it when you reshell and is tracked in your archetype instead of your shell.

docs.title: PC Trauma
docs.text:
When a character suffers trauma, fill 1 segment of your trauma clock on your archetype handout. Then make the Suffer trauma move with a base dice pool equal to your total trauma (the number of segments filled in your trauma clock).

[TODO: Add play examples]

docs.title: NPC Trauma
docs.text: When a NPC suffers trauma, the MC decides what happens to them. They may loose their shit all together or it may change their personality in subtle ways. They may go in a blind rage or run away never to be seen again. In any case, when a NPC suffers trauma, the MC is free to go hard on them.

docs.title: Harm
docs.text: Harm represents the physical damage inflicted to characters. As a tag, harm is noted n-harm.

docs.title: Harm clock
docs.text: Shells have a harm clock which represent its physical integrity. At character creation, the harm clock have 6 unfilled tracks.

docs.title: Suffering harm
docs.text:
As a PC, when you suffer harm : 1. Substract your n-armor to the n-harm suffered. If the harm has the ap tag (armor penetration), ignore this step. 2. Roll the Suffer harm move with the harm suffered (minus armor if applied) as your dice pool and establish consequences according to the move. 3. Fill as many tracks of your Harm Clock as the harm suffered.

If your harm clock is at 4 or more, you're in a critical state. Your state will worsen unless you get appropriate help. If your harm clock goes to 6, your shell dies. What happens next depends on the situation and your resurrection insurance.

docs.title: Healing/Repairing harm
docs.text:
By default, it costs 1-cred to remove the first 3 segments of your harm clock. For each additional segment past the first 3, it cost +1-cred.

Repairing a broken aug costs 1-cred for each aug repaired.

docs.title: NPC Harm
docs.text:
When NPC suffers harm, the same rules for armor and ap apply but NPC don't make moves and the consequence of harm is determined by this scale :

  • 0-harm : Concussions.
  • 1-harm : Blood.
  • 2-harm : May be fatal if left unchecked.
  • 3-harm : Likely fatal.
  • 4-harm : Fatal and gore.
  • 5-harm and more : Bodily destructive.

docs.title: Things Harm
docs.text:
For small objects, if you may have what it takes to destroy them, you destroy them, no problem. Most things will be destroyed with 1-harm or 2-harm this way.

For vehicles and buildings, consider that only harm with the breach tag will add up while normal harm doesn't stack up.

To destroy bigger things like entire arcologies and habitats, you will need special measures and arrangements.

docs.title: Making moves
docs.text:
When you have to roll the dice to make a move:

  1. Get the associated stat as base dice pool.
  2. +1d if your action is boosted and -1d if it's disrupted
  3. Add or remove dice based on forward or ongoing modifiers from situations or gear.
  4. +1d if you accept a basilisk bargain.
  5. Roll the dice pool !

If the best dice is a 6 : Success. If it's a 4 or 5 : Partial. If it's a 1, 2 or 3 : Miss. Refer to the move to see what happens. If the MC announces that the roll is on the edge, a partial or a miss will bring additional complications.

Move is a general term describing snippets of rules that players have access to. Some moves may give you straight boons to do certain things, some moves allow you to do things that are otherwise impossible, but the really interesting moves are the ones where you have to roll the dice.

Moves have a trigger which is the condition needed for the move to happen. For instance, the Convince move has the following trigger : "When you argue, fast talk or persuade". This means that the move triggers whenever a PC engage another character by arguing, fast talking or persuading them. When the trigger is met, then a roll will determine what happens next.

Moves requiring a roll all have 3 outcome levels : success, partial and miss. A success means that the PC accomplishes what they intend to do. A partial means that the PC accomplishes what they intend to do, but at a cost. A miss means the PC does not accomplishes what they intend to do and, additionally, something bad occurs. It's important to be aware that a partial, while having a cost, is still a hit and not a failure. This means that as the MC you should not make it feel like a failure. On a partial, the PC did what they intended, the cost is something that adds to the situation.

docs.title: Rolling the dice
docs.text:
In Silicon Dust, you roll a pool of 6-sided dice. When you need to roll the dice, you first need to create your dice pool.

When a move that requires a roll is triggered, look first for the associated stat. Your base dice pool contains as many dice as the move associated stat. For instance, the move Deploy violence requires to roll +Kinesics. On your shell sheet, look at your Kinesics stat and add as many dice to your pool. While most moves use the default 4 stats (Kinesics, Memetics, Process and Sensors), some moves use other mechanics such as Trauma, Harm, Rep, Lifestyle, ...

Next, look at potential bonus and malus. The first kind of bonus or malus comes from the Boost / Disrupt move triggered by another PC. Basically, if they help you, the give you +1d or if they interfere with you, they give you -1d. In all cases they must tell how they help you and they are entangled in the situation and will suffer the consequences alongside you. Only one boost and one disrupt may be in play for a given move, they cannot stack.

Another kind of bonus may come from special gear and assets. For instance, you may have CQC softwares giving you +1d to deploy violence in a close combat situation.

Some moves may also give you +1d-forward, meaning the next relevant action is given a bonus of +1 die. Other moves may give +1d-ongoing, meaning you have +1 die while the fictional aspect giving you this bonus is relevant. You may also encounter their negative counterparts -1d-forward and -1d-ongoing which remove one die from your pool. Both forward and ongoing modifiers go out of scope when their fictional counterparts is not relevant anymore. The difference is that forward modifiers can be used only once while ongoing modifiers may be used for several rolls. To keep track of forward and ongoing modifiers it's useful to use tokens with different colors or pieces of paper.

Finally, you may ask for a basilisk's bargain. It consists of a consequence that will happen in addition to whatever the move says. The consequence of a basilisk's bargains will happen independently of your outcome level, which means that you will suffer this consequence even on a success. Anyone may offer a basilisk's bargain, including the PC making the move, but it's ultimately the MC's choice to decide which bargain is acceptable and it's the PC's choice to accept or refuse the bargain. If you accept it, you gain +1d, if you refuse it, no bonus die.

[TODO: Add play examples]

You should now have your dice pool. Roll them !

A roll is a success if the highest die of your pool is a 6. It's a partial if the highest die is a 4 or 5. And it's a miss if the highest die is 1, 2 or 3.

If you have 0 dice in your pool, you may still roll ! Simply roll 2 dice but look at the lowest die of the two instead of the highest.

Go back to your move under the appropriate outcome level and see what happens.

[TODO: Add play examples]

Rolling the dice :

  1. Select your move and get the associated stat bonus as base dice pool.
  2. If you're move is boosted by another PC, gain +1d. If your move is disrupted by another PC, loose -1d.
  3. If a gear or asset gives a bonus die, add it to the pool.
  4. If you have any relevant forward or ongoing modifiers, add and remove die as established.
  5. If you accept a basilisk's bargain, gain +1d and suffer the consequence.
  6. Roll your dice pool. If the highest die is a 6, it's a success. If the highest die is a 4 or 5, it's a partial. If the highest die is a 1, 2 or 3, it's a miss.

docs.title: Resolving moves
docs.text:
When you have rolled the dice and know the resulting outcome level of your roll, you need to read the move to see what happens.

For instance, if you rolled a partial on Infiltrate, then refer to the section under partial in this move :

Partial : You go unnoticed but the MC chooses 1 :

  • You attract minor attention.
  • You leave something behind.
  • You take something with you.
  • You have to overcome an obstacle.

"You go unnoticed" for sure because the move says so, but the MC may choose that "You have to overcome an obstacle" ! So he may say something like "Sure you go unnoticed, but the vents lead you to a high speed fan blocking the way. What do you do ?".

Moves with outcome levels are sometimes very explicit and strict in the outcome (e.g. "You go unnoticed") and sometimes they need a bit of interpretation (e.g. "You have to overcome an obstacle"). Usually, it's the MC's job to interpret the evocative parts of moves, but anyone can propose ideas based on the fictional situation at hand.

[TODO: Add play examples]

docs.title: On the edge
docs.text:
Sometimes, before you rolled the dice, the MC may say that your roll is on the edge. Mechanically, it's the same as a normal roll, but with a little caveat. On a partial or miss, the MC may make an additional hard complication against you. What this means is that you make your roll as usual, you resolve the consequences of the resulting outcome level as described in your move, and then, the MC chooses one of its hard complications in addition to what the move says.

On the edge rolls are a MC tool to frame your action as especially risky. It doesn't reduce your dice pool, meaning it doesn't change your probabilities of succeeding, but in case of a partial or miss, there will be severe consequences. You've been warned.

[TODO: Add play examples]

docs.title: Shells
docs.text:
With the digital mind comes the realization that our bodies are mere shells, replaceable, a commodity. Bodies used to be extensively augmented but now we change them at will, we reshell. We design them conform to our cultural identities, our preferences. Shells are mirrors of our paradigms.

Shells are one of the core concepts of characters. They represent your bodies and their abilities. Shell are composed of several components:

  • Stats, Kinesics, Memetics, Process and Sensors, which are the most common kind of bonuses for moves.
  • Augs, which represents the various augmentations of this particular shell.
  • A Harm Clock, to track how much harm this shell has suffered.
  • A type and a look, which allows generation of aesthetic flavor for your shell.
  • A downside, every shell has at least one.

docs.title: Stats
docs.text:
There are 4 main stats in Silicon Dust :

  • Kinesics means the precision, speed and strength of your shell.
  • Memetics means the social utilities embedded in your shell.
  • Process means the pure computational power of your MPU.
  • Sensors means the quality and diversity of your shell's sensory capabilities.

These 4 stats may go from +0 to +4 where +0 is the base human level. By default they're at 0 but you may buy stats as augs to increase their associated bonus. See Making moves for more details on how to use these stats.

docs.title: Augs
docs.text:
Augs, short for augmentations, are technological improvements from base human.

  • Augs can be acquired by spending cred during ellipsis.
  • Augs can be broken. (for stat augs, it means -1).

docs.title: Harm Clock
docs.text:
A shell is a physical object susceptible to destruction and as such your shell tracks the amount of harm it suffered. When a shell's Harm Clock is full, this shell is forever destroyed and you automatically make the Resurrection insurance move.

See Harm for more details.

docs.title: Type and look
docs.text:
There are 3 types of shells :

  • Bioshells : biological shells, most are fast grown in vats, but some still from an uterus (or a cloaca, or an egg...). While the humanoid form is endlessly declined, uplifted and chimera templates also exist on the market.
  • Synthshells : synthetic shells, made from metal, plastic, silicon, usually robot looking. The design varies wildly but and almost all are marked by the stigma of lower quality as the sensory experience of synthshells usually don't have the "organic" quality of their biologic counterparts.
  • Infoshells : not really a shell, more like a custom server somewhere and you present as a digital avatar. You exist only on the Grid but you may integrate in augmented reality in most habitats.

In your shell handout, your type determines a list of options to determine your look.

docs.title: Downside
docs.text:
Finally, all shells have a downside. Your shell's downside can be a lot of things and functions as fictional positioning : it states something true in the fiction. At character creation, players choose their character's shell downside. When changing shells, whether because you buy a new one or your resurrection insurance activates.

This list, also found in the Resurrection insurance move, may help you to find an interesting downside : tracked, previous owner, corporate override, unresponsive, buggy perception, addiction, wild controls, psycho-affective trouble, chronic pain, ...

[TODO: add play examples]

docs.title: Resurrection insurance & Reshell
docs.text: Your shell may die. When that happens, your Resurrection insurance kicks in and you have to make hard choices on your new shell. When it's done, you may Reshell, meaning reuploading your mind in this new shell.

docs.title: Transhumanity
docs.text:
Transhumanity has never been this diverse both in their minds and in their bodies.

We're in a far future where cutting edge technology now is historical. Advances in genetics allowed us to upgrade our genes and create artificial bodies and creatures. With programmable nanotechnology, transhumanity

docs.title: Immortality
docs.text: Due to the invention of mind digitization technology, transhumans are now immortals. Their immortality resides in the fact that their neural topology can be finely monitored and stored as data, allowing them to be resurrected in case their body dies. If dead, this brain snapshot can be put in a new body or run in a virtual environment on a computer. Almost everyone has, as a basic augmentation, a cortical stack that acts as this internal backup, constantly monitoring and updating its data to contain to the latest events experienced. Because of this technology, new terminology came around, the digitized extended mind was called the ego and the replaceable bodies were called shells. In addition, it is not unusual to have backups in cold storage at home or in resurrection insurance mindbanks in case your cortical stack has been destroyed. There are all manner of policies regarding these past backups but usually after some time, the most recent backup is resurrected and is considered the person that died. Usually this happens rarely though, only when people have their cortical stack destroyed and not connected to the Grid. Death now refers to the destruction of the shell and permadeath has come to refer to the permanent destruction of all backups of an ego.

docs.title: Forks
docs.text:
The logical conclusion of mind digitization was copying oneself by putting a backup in additional shells. This is known as forking and transhumans created this way are known as forks. The limit between a fork and a resurrected individual is a complex legal and metaphysical debate and changes between polities. Usually, forking is illegal because of the demographic explosion risk associated to it. There are many exceptions to this rule but it is often done in the shadows. Forks and the original are often forced to merge or to suicide themselves until only one remains.

Mechanically, when a player character forks themselves, they make the Fork move. In that case, they may control and play their multiple forks. While a player character has forks, they must occasionally make the Diverge move to see if they loose narrative control of one of their forks.

docs.title: Pseudoforks
docs.text: Pseudoforks are simple AI updated with basic models of your beliefs, motivations and personnality. They come in various levels of refinements and are used to send representatives, whether because of a busy schedule or to overcome speed of light latency between distant habitats. Advanced pseudoforks are usually illegal as there have been cases of pseudoforks going rogue. They are usually hunted done in that case.

docs.title: Psychosurgery
docs.text:
With the ego digitized, it was possible to edit it as data, an art known as psychosurgery. Minds are now upgraded with artificial networks integrated using psychosurgery. Psychosurgery also allows to add and remove memories, beliefs, emotions. Transhumanity is able to intentionally edit its own mind. Some only edit out minor bad memories but some have edited so much as they are barely recognizable as human minds. Advanced and experimental psychosurgery may lead to trauma and psychiatric diseases like paranoïa, delusions, personality changes, depression, ... It also allowed to create new kinds of minds called neuromorphs.

Mechanically, for player characters, acts of psychosurgery may be performed by having the Psychosurgery expertise or acquiring the services of an expert with these skills.

docs.title: Neuromorphs
docs.text: Base: Only minor modifications. Construct: Not so much a specific kind of neuromorph, but serves to identify fully artificial minds, whatever their inner architecture. They are usually simply called AI and rarely admitted to legal personhood in most habitats. Immutables: They disabled neural plasticity in their value centers and deleted most or all pathways related to emotions, they also sometimes deleted all their memories from before. They're known to be cold, devoted to a cause and incorruptible. Sometimes becoming an immutable is required by polities and corporations for some roles. They often are argumentative and exhaustive in conversations. Precogs: They wove many feedback pathways between unrelated parts of their brain. They are known to be hypersensitive, intuitive and unreliable. They are creative and improvisational. They often speak in strange metaphors and aphorisms. Uplift: They were animals before being uplifted to sentience. They added metacognition, planning and higher order knowledge to their animal brain. They still have desires and behaviors of their previous mindless animals. Uplifiting is rare and highly regulated in most polities. Seraphims: They're a rare and experimental agglomerate of multiple forks of the same individual. Illegal everywhere except in some outer habitats. Crowdmind: Similar to seraphims but the agglomerate contains different people that fused. They experience every point of view at the same time allowing them to form more abstract thoughts about reality. They talk in generality and are often dedicated to a communal cause. They're illegal almost everywhere except in their own habitats or in highly unregulated habitats.

docs.title: Shells and augs
docs.text:
Shells are the replaceable bodies of transhumanity. They exist in many forms but are classified under 3 main categories: bioshells are mainly made of organic materials, synthshells are mainly made of synthetic materials and infoshells are not really bodies, they're servers running egos in simulspaces.

Shells are usually upgraded by various augs, short for augmentations. They range from

Shells are specialized

docs.title: Reproduction
docs.text: Because of immortality, the laws quickly changed in order to avoid a demographic explosion. Permits to reproduce must be issued by most polities to have children in order to control the demographics of habitats. Unlicensed children often requires parents to be forcefully reshelled in infoshells to pay for the ressources

docs.title: The Grid
docs.text: The Grid is the transhuman evolution of internet. From internet it kept the concept of being a communication network. But the Grid is much more than that. It's an ecosystem of various subnetworks, communications systems, protocols and habitat and things meshes all connected to one another through gateways and backdoors. The Grid varies from habitat to habitat. Habitat subnetworks come in all shades of surveillance, transparency, encryption level, anonymity, accessibility, services, reputation systems, ... In habitat with fascistic tendencies it may be hard to connect to the rest of the Grid, you may need a contact with access to a stealth satellite or admin access. In others, the Grid may easily be accessed but is easily traceable if you do not take proper precautions. In free habitats, anonymity may be a protocol-level feature allowing for free and anonymous access to the Grid.

docs.title: Grid inserts
docs.text: Everyone, or almost everyone, has grid inserts in their shell, which are a set of augmentations designed to wirelessly connect to the Grid and interface it with your sensory systems. Grid inserts lets you and your angel access information on the grid, add layers of holograms in your perception and enter simulspaces.

docs.title: Accessing the Grid
docs.text: Some systems offer free and secured access to the Grid, but others have ego verification measures and automatic surveillance AIs. In general, even if there is an authoritarian regime in charge, there is always some resistance running micro-satellites providing a connection to the rest of the Grid, you just have to find them. Note that because of the distance between stellar bodies, the Grid is asynchronous. Data from Pluto may not be accessible immediately from Mercury. However, [...]

docs.title: Alternate reality and the Mesh
docs.text:
The Grid is also composed of layers and layers of virtual elements covering the physical reality of habitats. Your perception of habitats is enriched of holographic displays, spatialized sounds and even virtual taste and flavor. Parts of this alternate reality comes from your applications : a map application will display a minimap in your view field or indicate markers in the environment, social utils and network clients may print a data bubbles and public profiles of people surrounding you, weapon detection systems may highlight detected weapon in your view field, ... Another layer of alternate reality comes from your direct environment. Most habitat structures and objects are directly interconnected forming the Mesh : the aggregate of embedded data and utilities. Doors may give you access data, the lightning system of rooms may be accessible, holographic advertising and tags cover the walls, weapons may print information such as the remaining ammo and the coils temperature, ... Off-grid, society looks weird, people talk to invisible people, they make movements in the air, they seem to see things that are not there, ...

When you create an habitat, ask yourself what is the basic experience of the local mesh ? Is it standardized or diverse ? Minimalistic or saturated ? Functional or aesthetic ?

When you create your character, ask yourself how you configure your alternate reality settings ? What utilities you always have directly available ? What kind of experiences your angel filters or lets through in your perception ?

docs.title: Virtual reality and simulspaces
docs.text: Another aspect of the everyday experience of transhumanity is virtual reality. Simulspaces are artificial virtual spaces allowing seemingly real experiences by transmitting perceptual data directly into grid inserts. Simulspaces of various sizes, populations and designs coexist in the Grid. Some are huge immersive games, others are virtual offices for corporations, others are artificial landscapes for virtual tourism, others are individual relaxation spaces, others are huge social cities, ... What you can do in simulspace depends on its programming. Some sandbox simulspaces give a lot of possibilities to build things but others only lets you move. The physics of simulspaces may be very realistic or outright weird with non-euclidean physics or anything in between. Simulspace time may go faster than real time but there may be limits to that. All shells allow to create their own personal simulspaces and grid inserts allow people to access simulspaces on the Grid. While you may be blocked doing things in simulspaces, you may always log out as long as you have control of your grid inserts. However, an cortical stack running in an egoloader may not be able to log out allowing for effective accelerated torture.

docs.title: Society
docs.text: Human societies evolved into transhuman societies. ego technology removed the fear of death and profoundly shifted the spiritual values. Old religions couldn't keep up with the leap and new religions emerged. After the Fall, old states perished leaving the corporations taking the Overton window. They associated under the name of the Consortium.

docs.title: Economy
docs.text:
AI automation and the progress of science allowed for production chains capable of producing most goods for cheap and in mass. However, except on earth where everything is available, other habitats on the system depends on costly imports on the ressources they lack locally. On Venus, air is cheap but water is expensive for instance. This ripples locally on individual habitat economies.

So everyone still has to work.

docs.title: Habitats
docs.text:
An habitat is an artificial construct designed for transhuman to live in. Habitats come in various shapes and functionnalities :

  • Aerostats are mobile stations supported by floating balloons in the dangerous atmospheres of Venus, Jupiter and Saturn. Aerostats benefits from the natural bodies of their planets. They use atmosphere processors to convert the external atmosphere into usable raw material but they always need mining importations for hard materials. On Venus, aerostats descend towards the planet surface at mining facilities to reload their stocks.
  • Beehives are habitats drilled in asteroids. They consists of connected clusters of corridors and rooms. Beehives are interface with the void of space through pressurized locks. Most beehives have an external spaceport whethere as a small intellidome or a hard-material building. As they are built in asteroids, they usually are microgravity environments and consequently have confusing 3D architecture for transhumans not used to 0-g designs. Some modern beehives control and are built as arrays of concentric circles to use the asteroid rotation as artificial gravity. Beehives first use the rock and ice of their host asteroid as raw material for fabrication.
  • Intellidomes are habitats built on terrestrial planets and asteroids. They consist of several layers of programmable nanotech that separates the dome internal atmosphere from the void of space or the planet's atmosphere. These programmable layers allow for shuttles with authorization to enter and exit the dome by opening and closing themselves in order to minimize atmospheric losses while allowing shuttles to come and go. Intellidomes are not always domes, they can be of various shapes but the term comes from early adoptions of this technology. Intellidomes can selectively filter light and radiation in order to protect its population and to recreate day-night cycles. They come in various sizes, from small stations of a few dozen meters to large cities of a few kilometers.
  • O'Neill cylinders are huge nanocarbon cylinders rotating around their central axis to provide artificial gravity. Habitations are built [...]
  • Orbitals are habitats orbiting around a massive planetary or stellar body. Orbital stations are orbiting around a singular body, usually a planet while Lagrange stations are located at the stable lagrange points of some planet orbits.

Characterize habitat (choose 3 to 5) :

  • 0g.
  • Archipelagos.
  • Artificial gravity.
  • Asteroids.
  • Baroque.
  • Centralized.
  • Chaotic.
  • Cramped.
  • DIY.
  • Domes.
  • Fancy.
  • Functional.
  • Gated.
  • Highly secured.
  • In bad shape.
  • Industrial.
  • Lots of vegetation.
  • Minimalist.
  • Satellites.
  • Spacious.
  • Special atmosphere.
  • Surveillance.
  • Sturdy.
  • Vertical.

docs.title: Sol
docs.text:
Habitats : Habitats in sol's corona are space stations protected by a tear-shaped shell generating a strong electromagnetic field and providing an effective cooling system. Habitats cost a lot because of the difficulty to maintain them.

Resources : Energy.

Needs : Everything else.

docs.title: Mercury
docs.text:
Habitats : Mercury habitats are intellidomes and underground stations mostly located near the poles and inside the curves of asteroid craters to benefit of the best protection against the sun.

Resources : Low Gravity, Magnetosphere, Metals, Solar energy.

Needs : Volatiles, Water.

docs.title: Venus
docs.text:
Habitats : Venus habitats are aerostats floating in the atmosphere. On the surface, some mining facilities are built to resist Venus surface extreme conditions. There are also some space stations at the stable lagrangians.

Resources : Volatiles, Water, Gravity, Induced magnetosphere, Metals, Solar and Wind energy.

docs.title: Earth
docs.text: Earth has been destroyed during WWIII. Earth is unliveable.

docs.title: Luna
docs.text:
Resources : Low-gravity, Metals.

Needs : Volatiles, Water.

docs.title: Mars
docs.text:
Mars is concurrent to Venus to

Resources : Water, Gravity, Metals, Volatiles.

docs.title: Asteroid's belt
docs.text:
Resources : Water, Metals.

Needs : Volatiles.

docs.title: Jupiter
docs.text:
Resources : Volatiles.

Needs : Water, Metals.

docs.title: Saturn
docs.text:
Resources : Volatiles.

Needs : Water, Metals.

docs.title: Uranus
docs.text:
Resources : Volatiles.

Needs : Water, Metals.

docs.title: Neptun
docs.text:
Resources : Volatiles.

Needs : Water, Metals.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
The setting in Silicon Dust has both fixed and moving parts. Fixed parts are the prevalence of mind uploading technology, brain-computer interfaces. Somewhat moving parts are space colonization and augmentations. Free moving parts are the presence of extraterrestrial intelligence, the existence of Gates or the happening of major historical events. Every aspects developed in Transhumanity is a necessary part of the setting, but other aspects may be introduced.

For this purpose, here are setting modules to be combined to create a setting. Setting modules are a sets of facts and questions for you to help create a rich and interesting setting.

To determine which module to use to help create the setting, answer these questions and refer to the corresponding modules.

  • Does transhumanity lives :
  • still mainly on Earth ? -> Old Earth
  • mainly in the solar system -> Sol
  • among many stellar systems -> Terran expansion
  • Are there aliens ? -> Aliens.
  • Are there Gates ? -> Gates.
  • Did transhumanity experienced an existential risk that nearly made it extinct ? -> The Fall.

docs.title: Old Earth
docs.text:
Play the Old Earth if you want a more traditional Gibson-like cyberpunk, but with egotechnology. Here adventures take place in sprawling futuristic cities controlled by hypercorporations and characters are ants in gigantic social systems bound to crush them. This settings also allows to explore how Earth was transformed by hypercapitalism.

docs.title: The Fall
docs.text:
Play The Fall setting if you want to explore themes such as transhumanity survival and adaptation in the solar system, transhumanity's development in awareness of existential threats, shifting power relations between corporations and other weirder societies, singularities, nanotechnologies, potential first contacts, near space exploration, ... This is the default setting for Silicon Dust.

Earth has fallen and with it, humanity. Only transhumanity survived the Fall and they do not dare come back to Earth. Instead, they colonized the solar system, filling it with habitats. The Consortium, a plutocratic corpocracy, has taken the Overton window and reigns over the inner system. Elsewhere, multiple polities more or less coexist : neocommunist utopias where currency was replaced by reputation, anarcho-capitalist habitats where you need a paid subscription for oxygen and legal representation, exotic and deadly exhuman settlements, theocratic singularity seekers, ... While transhumanity survived, the memory of the Fall is still fresh and some prepare for a second and definitive Fall.

Why did Earth fell ? (choose 1 or more) :

  • Alien invasion.
  • Artificial intelligences gone rogue.
  • Earth suddenly vanished.
  • Fundamental physics accident.
  • Global warming.
  • It is not entirely understood.
  • Vicious autoreplicating nanotechnology.
  • World War III.

Worldbuilding options (choose as many as you line) :

  • An alien civilization made first contact recently.
  • An intelligent physical and virtual virus from unknown origin is plaguing the system.
  • Earth is surrounded by a belt of killsats forbidding access. No one knows who put them there.
  • Earth is filled with the autoreplicating remains of war.
  • Gates were found in the solar system.
  • Consortium is waging an open war again the outer polities.
